coredata.dc:title: Nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Guojian
Qiu, Huimin
Liu, Xin
Luo, Pengjie
Wu, Yongning
Shen, Yizhong
coredata.dc:description:
Background Pathogenic bacterial infections have become a global concern for safety, and pathogens in food-related matrices can cause a wide range of diseases (e.g., common cold, asthma, influenza, tuberculosis, anthrax and pneumonia). Therefore, it is necessary to enhance the accuracy of monitoring pathogenic bacteria levels in the food-related matrices. However, traditional detection methods are often complex and time-consuming. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays have been widely used by researchers due to their high fluorescence intensity, stability, low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity.
Scope and approach To promote further development in this field, we review here the identification of nanomaterials with pathogenic bacteria based on fluorescent assays and the progress of research on the detection of pathogenic bacteria using functionalized nanoparticles (gold nanomaterials, silver nanomaterials, quantum dots, upconversion nanomaterials, magnetic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials and metal-organic frameworks/covalent organic frameworks). Finally, we discuss the shortcomings of fluorescent assays and provide current challenges and prospects for the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices.
Key finds and conclusions The use of nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays in the detection of pathogenic bacteria has broken the limitations of traditional methods, resulting in much shorter detection time and much higher detection sensitivity. However, detection applications are still at the laboratory stage, and future research should focus on the development of high-throughput detection techniques and portable or visual fluorescent sensors for commercial and industrial applications. We envision that this review will contribute to the development of more sensitive, accurate and convenient nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices for food and public safety.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices.

AuNCs-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of E. coli using AuNCs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of the AuNCs solution incubated with Cu2+ and E. coli with various concentrations for 0.5 h. (C) Linear calibration curve between AuNCs fluorescence intensity change and bacterial concentration. Reproduced with permission (Yan, et al., 2018). Copyright 2018, American Chemical Society.

Silver nanocluster-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of S. typhimurium using AgNCs. Reproduced with permission (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2017). Copyright 2017, Elsevier. (B) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of E. coli using DNA-based fluorescent silver nanocluster and MNP-DNAzyme-AChE complex. Reproduced with permission (Zheng, et al., 2018). Copyright 2018, Elsevier. (C) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of S. typhimurium using DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs. Reproduced with permission (Fu, Yang, et al., 2022). Copyright 2022, MDPI. (D) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of S. typhimurium using DNA-AgNCs. Reproduced with permission (Yang, et al., 2021). Copyright 2021, Elsevier.

QDs-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of E. coli O157:H7 using a bilayer channel of magnetic nanoparticles and quantum dots. (B) The spectra of the fluorescent E. coli O157:H7 at different concentrations. (C) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of E. coli O157:H7. Reproduced with permission (Xue, et al., 2018). Copyright 2018, Elsevier.

UCNPs-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of S. aureus using UCNPs-aptamer donor and cDNA-BHQ-1 acceptor. (B) The recovery of upconversion fluorescence in the presence of different concentrations of S. aureus. (C) Standard curve of relative fluorescence intensity at 540 nm versus the concentration of target S. aureus. Reproduced with permission (Liu, Yu, et al., 2022). Copyright 2022, Elsevier.

Magnetic nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of Salmonella using HCR amplification and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Reproduced with permission (Yu, et al., 2019). Copyright 2019, Elsevier. (B) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of E. coli and S. aureus using Mag-CDs. Reproduced with permission (Bhaisare, et al., 2016). Copyright 2016, Elsevier.

Carbon nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria. (A) Schematic diagram of fluorescent assays of S. aureus using Apt-Van-QDs@CNPs. ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: LOD is defined as the limit of detection and is usually expressed as 3σ/k (where σ means the standard deviation of 11 blank samples and k is the slope of linearity of the linear equation). LOD serves as an indicator of sensitivity and the lower the LOD detected, the higher the sensitivity.

Abbreviations: AuNC: Gold nanoclusters; AuNPs: Gold nanoparticles; E. coli: Escherichia coli; CeO2 NPs: CeO2 nanoparticles; MNPs: Magnetic nanoparticles; S. typhimurium: Salmonella typhimurium; UCNPs: Up-conversion nanoparticles; QDs: Quantum dots; L. monocytogenes: Listeria monocytogenes; AgNCs: Silver nanoclusters; S. aureus: Staphylococcus aureus; V. parahemolyticus: Vibrio parahaemolyticus; C. sakazakii: Cronobacter sakazakii; S. flexneri: Shigella flexneri; CDs: Carbon dots; CQDs: Carbon quantum dots; MNBs: Magnetic nanobeads; D. desulfuricans: Desulfovibrio desulfuricans; P. aeruginosa: Pseudomonas aeruginosa; S. sciuri: Staphylococcus sciuri; CNPs: Carbon nanoparticles; NFs: Nanofibers; BCDs: Blue carbon dots; GQDs: Graphene quantum dots; GO: Graphene oxide; S. enteritidis: Streptococcus enteritidis NCND: Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots; NG: Nano graphite; Tb - MOF: Terbium metal-organic framework; Cd-MOF: Cadmium metal-organic framework; S. albus: Streptomyces albus; Eu-MOF: Europium metal−organic framework.

head.ce:title: Nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices
