ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pathogenic bacterial infections have become a global concern for safety, and pathogens in food-related matrices can cause a wide range of diseases (e.g., common cold, asthma, influenza, tuberculosis, anthrax and pneumonia). Therefore, it is necessary to enhance the accuracy of monitoring pathogenic bacteria levels in the food-related matrices. However, traditional detection methods are often complex and time-consuming. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays have been widely used by researchers due to their high fluorescence intensity, stability, low cost, high sensitivity and selectivity.

To promote further development in this field, we review here the identification of nanomaterials with pathogenic bacteria based on fluorescent assays and the progress of research on the detection of pathogenic bacteria using functionalized nanoparticles (gold nanomaterials, silver nanomaterials, quantum dots, upconversion nanomaterials, magnetic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials and metal-organic frameworks/covalent organic frameworks). Finally, we discuss the shortcomings of fluorescent assays and provide current challenges and prospects for the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices.

The use of nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays in the detection of pathogenic bacteria has broken the limitations of traditional methods, resulting in much shorter detection time and much higher detection sensitivity. However, detection applications are still at the laboratory stage, and future research should focus on the development of high-throughput detection techniques and portable or visual fluorescent sensors for commercial and industrial applications. We envision that this review will contribute to the development of more sensitive, accurate and convenient nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices for food and public safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, contamination with pathogenic bacteria in the food-related matrices has become one of the major causes of human health problems. Most of the world's known diseases are caused by different pathogenic bacteria such as Streptococcus, E. coli O157:H7, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Listeria monocytogenes, and Salmonella plague (Chen, Andler, Goddard, Nugen, & Rotello, 2017). According to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), more than 45 million people in the Americas are sickened each year by pathogenic bacteria in the food-related matrices, with up to 120,000 hospitalizations, more than 3000 deaths, and $15 billion in economic losses, resulting in serious consequences (Ali, Altemimi, Alhelfi, & Ibrahim, 2020). The presence of pathogenic bacteria and their ability to survive bring current protective measures used for food-related matrices into serious question, and timely detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices is essential, but there are few options for rapid diagnostic tests. There are a number of pathogenic bacteria detection methods available, but they all have certain problems. For example, plate counting is simple to perform, has high specificity and sensitivity, and is widely used for bacterial detection, but is laborious, time-consuming, and requires specific skills (Ma, et al., 2021). Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) is highly accurate, but requires specialized instruments and personnel to operate and is prone to erroneous results (Cao, Yu, Dong, Chen, & Zhang, 2020). Serological methods, such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA), are based on specific antigen-antibody interactions and can detect different species of bacteria, but the reagents required are generally more expensive (Majdinasab, Hayat, & Marty, 2018). The colorimetric method can be viewed directly with the naked eye, simplifying the process, but the detection sensitivity is relatively low (Cai, Jung, Bhadra, & Ellington, 2018). Therefore, the development of an alternative method for rapid and accurate detection of pathogenic bacteria is of great importance to safeguard public and food safety and ultimately to defeat infectious diseases.

Fluorescent assays refers to an analysis method in which certain substances produce fluorescence under the irradiation of light of a specific wavelength, and use its intensity to perform qualitative or quantitative analysis of substances (Xiao, et al., 2023). Compared with traditional pathogenic bacteria assays, fluorescent assays has many advantages such as ultra-low detection limit, high sensitivity, greater detection range, good selectivity, low cost, etc. (Walekar, et al., 2017). In the early days, traditional fluorescent dyes were commonly used as fluorescent agents, but fluorescent dyes have narrow excitation spectrum, high background, broad fluorescence emission spectrum, easy to be photobleached and other defects, which greatly limit their application in the field of analysis (Yoon, Park, Cha, Gopala, & Lee, 2021). With the development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials are widely used for fluorescent assays due to their unique optical, physical and chemical properties, enabling some of the aforementioned problems to be solved (Hassan, et al., 2022; Y. Shen et al., 2021). At the same time, nanomaterials have made possible the miniaturization of detection devices, resulting in fast, accurate, efficient, sensitive and portable pathogen diagnostic systems that can be operated by non-technical personnel to measure target parameters in the field using a small number of samples (Sai-Anand, et al., 2019). For example, Zhang et al. used target identification of aptamers and copper nanoparticles (CuNPs) to establish a fluorescent assays, achieved ultra-sensitive detection of live Streptococcus enteritidis down to 25.0 CFU mL−1 (P. Zhang, Khan, et al., 2020). In addition, Zhao et al. used magnetic particles and quantum dots with different fluorescence emission wavelengths to simultaneously detect three foodborne pathogenic bacteria (S. typhimurium, Shigella flexneri and E. coli O157:H7) simultaneously (Zhao, et al., 2009). Recently, Li et al. developed a two-step FRET system using platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and Ru–S3, which successfully detected S. aureus (Li, Xu, Feng, Zhang, & Miao, 2023). From these successful examples above, it is not difficult to find that nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays have great potential for the detection of pathogenic bacteria. Currently, there are three main strategies for the detection of pathogenic bacteria by nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays: (1) identification of bacteria by fluorescence imaging; (2) enhanced or altered fluorescence properties upon target binding; (3) bursting of fluorescent signals by energy receptors (Zhang, et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic tools for identifying pathogenic bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: In fluorescence assays for bacteria, recognition factors are important component of fluorescent assays and have a significant impact on the selectivity and limit of detection (LOD) of fluorescent assays. To obtain excellent detection results, the development of bacterial recognition elements with higher efficiency and specificity is much needed. In fluorescence assays for bacteria can be divided into specific and non-specific identification according to the different identification methods.

Specific biological recognition elements used for bacterial detection include mainly antibodies, enzymes, aptamer, whole cells, bacteriophages and antibiotics. They have a highly specific recognition of bacteria and can bring high specificity and sensitivity to the assay (Wang, et al., 2021).

Antibodies are commonly used recognition factor in fluorescent assays and are the most commonly used tools for capturing bacteria due to their outstanding specificity and strong binding ability. Based on their selective nature and the way they are synthesized, they can usually be classified as polyclonal antibodies, monoclonal antibodies and genetically engineered antibodies (Chen, Lin, et al., 2017). Antibodies typically utilize active sites on small molecules to chemically react with markers of amino, carboxyl, sulfhydryl, or hydroxyl groups on proteins - in short, to bind to a unique antigen in a highly specific manner. Recently, Pant et al. succeeded in achieving specific detection of E. coli through antigen-antibody interactions. As follows, antibody-conjugated cadmium telluride (CdTe) quantum dots were immobilized on the bacterial layer as a biorecognition target to specifically capture the target E. coli, which in turn generated fluorescent signals (Pant, et al., 2022). Although antibodies as recognition factors show many advantages in fluorescence detection, their poor stability, high price, easy loss of target antigen binding activity and susceptibility to external conditions during labelling limit their application in fluorescence detection (Tombelli, Minunni, & Mascini, 2007).

Enzymes are also widely used in fluorescent assays because they are highly specific for the substrate. In most cases, these enzymes are used in immunoassays to label antigens or antibodies, using the enzymes to catalyze the reaction of substrates to produce colored, luminescent or fluorescent products to detect the desired substance (Wang, Li, et al., 2020). Furthermore, high sensitivity and specificity of bacterial detection can be achieved by enzyme-dependent amplification in combination with other fluorescence analysis techniques. As a typical example, Leng et al. achieved ultrasensitive and specific detection of S. Typhimurium by combining polymerase-catalyzed bacterial bases with uracil DNA glycosylase (UDG) and endonuclease IV (Endo IV)-assisted enzyme repair amplification (ERA). The LOD of the method was 9.86 CFU mL−1 with a detection range of five orders of magnitude, which was 1000 times better compared to other existing methods (Leng, et al., 2017). Although this strategy showed satisfactory results, the enzyme activity is susceptible to environmental factors such as temperature and pH, which limited its application.

Aptamers are the main identifying element in the analysis of pathogenic bacteria. Aptamers are a class of oligonucleotides (RNA or DNA) synthesized by exponential enrichment (SELEX) from a large random oligonucleotide library through phylogenetic evolution of ligands. By folding into different secondary and tertiary structures, they can bind to a wide range of targets (Labuda, et al., 2010). Aptamers have been widely used in fluorescent assays because of their many distinct advantages over antibodies, including low synthesis cost, ease of use, multiple selective labeling, high target affinity and specificity, long-term storage stability, smaller size, non-toxicity and ease of modification. The aptamer has been successfully used to detect a variety of pathogenic bacteria such as Escherichia coli, Salmonella and Staphylococcus aureus (Zhang, Khan, et al., 2020). However, specific aptamer sequences often require a series of rigorous screening steps, and some aptamers can only be used to identify a single strain of a particular class of pathogenic bacteria, which has limitations in the diagnosis of bacterial infections.

Whole cells are also common recognition factor molecules in fluorescent assays (Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Nayak, et al., 2017). Using microbial cells as sensitive elements, whole-cell biosensors convert the collected correlation signals into readable signals and have been widely used in environmental pollutant monitoring, food safety detection, and disease diagnosis (Liu, Yu, et al., 2022). For example, Wu et al. constructed a novel whole-cell biosensor that successfully detected Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Burkholderia pseudomallei in contaminated water sources by responding to population sensing signals from target bacteria. (Wu, Wang, Wang, & Wei, 2021). More importantly, they can distinguish between live and dead bacteria, which is essential for bacterial detection. However, they require specific conditions to work, are difficult to store, and are prone to false-positive results, all of which require further improvement by researchers.

Bacteriophages are viruses that recognize and infect host bacteria and are natural enemies of bacteria, and they can be easily isolated from natural wastewater and soil. Not only do they cause host bacteria to divide, they also produce large numbers of viruses and are highly specific for their host bacteria. The excellent stability of bacteriophages under different temperature and pH conditions makes them highly advantageous biometric elements for fluorescent assays (van der Merwe, van Helden, Warren, Sampson, & van Pittius, 2014). Increasingly, more researchers are applying it to the fluorescence assays of bacteria. For example, Halkare et al. detected E. coli in wastewater using phage T4 label-free on a sensor. Specifically, bacteria were captured on the sensing platform, followed by the addition of their specific phage, which was free to spread and self-orient to bind to the bacterial surface captured on the sensing platform. And the detection of E. coli in lake water samples was realized by applying the principle of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) through gold nanoparticles (Halkare, et al., 2021). Furthermore, it can distinguish between dead and live bacteria at a lower cost because it can only interact specifically with live host bacteria. With this in mind, bacteriophages can be used to identify and distinguish between viable but non-culturable bacteria, something that is difficult for antibodies and aptamers to do (Ramamurthy, Ghosh, Pazhani, & Shinoda, 2014). However, this technique can only detect bacteria that can serve as phage hosts, which prevents it from being widely used.

Antibiotics is a class of secondary metabolites produced by microorganisms or higher plants and animals in the course of their lives that have anti-pathogenic or other activity and can interfere with the developmental functions of other living cells as chemical substances. Since some of them inhibit the activity of specific bacteria and can be covalently linked to small molecule fluorophores to form fluorescent antibiotics, they can also be used as recognition elements to detect bacteria by fluorescent assays under specific conditions. As a typical example, Phetsang et al. prepared an azide-functionalized oxazolidinone antibiotic that retained its antimicrobial activity and was further conjugated with fluorophores to form a fluorescent biotin probe, which was successfully fluorescently imaged against Gram-positive bacteria by using confocal microscopy (Phetsang, et al., 2014). Unlike aptamers that can only identify a single strain of a particular class of pathogenic bacteria, small molecule antibiotics can be used as molecular targets to effectively identify different types of bacterial groups and are somewhat versatile for distinguishing a particular class of pathogenic bacteria (You, Zhang, Wu, & Chen, 2019; Zhang et al., 2018).

Unlike previous specific identification, non-specific identification relies on the charged nature of the pathogen itself and its own structural characteristics. Although the sensitivity of non-specific identification is not as high as that of specific identification, non-specific identification is simple and inexpensive. Therefore, it is widely used in the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices. Electrostatic adsorption, as a non-specific identification method, can be used to detect a particular class of bacteria, or it can be used to detect specific bacteria based on their particular properties, which depend on the charged nature of the bacteria and the nanomaterials. When nanomaterials and bacteria have opposite charges on their surfaces, they can be bonded together by electrostatic adsorption and the bacteria will adsorb to their surfaces and absorb the fluorescent molecules, thus allowing the detection of bacteria (Tokonami & Iida, 2017). Recently, Escherichia coli, Salmonella, Cronobacter sakazakii, Shigella flexneri, Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Staphylococcus aureus, and Listeria monocytogenes have been successfully detected by electrostatic adsorption (Yin, et al., 2019).

Besides, Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria have significant differences in their outer wall composition, which can be utilised to develop appropriate non-specific detection strategies using differences in the outer cell wall. For example, lipopolysaccharide (LSP) is a component of the cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria, which can exhibit a variety of activities when interacting with other biological cells (Mobed & Hasanzadeh, 2022). Bao et al. used this property to construct a novel fluorescence assay. In the presence of Gram-negative bacteria, LPS specifically binds to polymyxin B to enhance the fluorescence signal and thus detect the bacteria. Furthermore, antimicrobial compounds, boric acid and carbohydrates are now widely used for bacterial identification (Bao, et al., 2021). Since many antibacterial drugs have the effect of binding specifically to the bacterial cell wall, they are widely used for bacterial detection, such as vancomycin can bind to the D-Ala-D-Ala peptide chain on the peptidoglycan of Gram-positive bacteria (Van Oosten et al., 2013). Boric acid is also used for the detection of bacteria because it selectively recognizes glycoproteins on biological surfaces (Lee, et al., 2020). Because bacteria can recognize and link glycolipids and glycoproteins formed by combining carbohydrates with lipids and proteins, they can also be used in the detection of bacteria. The identification of bacteria based on differences in cell wall composition is simple and efficient, but only one class of bacteria can be distinguished.

In addition to the non-specific direct identification of bacteria described above, bacteria can be detected indirectly by measuring the fluorescence signal or concentration of other relevant media in the system. For example, metabolites of bacteria, chemicals produced in the system, etc. Qi et al. synthesized CdS nanoparticles using sulfide-specific metabolic processes in the cells of sulfate-reducing bacteria using an environmentally friendly method, and the nanoparticles were used as fluorescent markers for bacterial detection in the range of 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 (Qi, Zhang, Zeng, & Wan, 2016). Although this method still needs to be improved, it certainly has some advantages in terms of detection time and convenience, and opens up a new avenue for bacterial detection (Zukovskaja, et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays toward pathogenic bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Residual pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices can enter the human body through physical contact or the food chain, thus posing a serious risk to environmental safety and human health. To reduce the harm caused by pathogenic bacteria, building an analytical method that can effectively monitor pathogenic bacteria in the food-related matrices will be of great importance. With the rapid development of nanotechnology, various nanomaterials are used for the detection of various pathogenic bacteria due to their unique electronic, magnetic and optical related properties, which can greatly improve the possibility of rapid and accurate detection of pathogenic bacteria. The new nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays have good detection performance for selective, fast and sensitive, easy to operate and real-time detection of pathogenic bacteria. To date, a number of analytical methods based on fluorescent nanomaterials have been used to effectively monitor pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices. Therefore, it is important to summarize and analyze examples of innovative nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacterial contamination, which will better assist researchers in developing novel, sensitive, rapid, and cost-effective assays. This safeguards food safety and human health. The main nanomaterials presented in this paper for fluorescent assays are gold nanomaterials, silver nanomaterials, quantum dots, up-conversion nanomaterials, magnetic nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials and metal-organic frameworks/covalent organic frameworks.

Nanoscale gold has unique optoelectronic, physical and chemical properties, therefore, among many nanomaterials research, gold nanomaterials, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), gold nanorods (AuNRs) and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are among the most researched and widely as well as intensively studied nanomaterials by researchers, and are widely used in building bio/chemical sensors, studying electrochemistry, optoelectronics, etc.(Khalilzadeh, et al., 2016; Majdalawieh, Kanan, El-Kadri, & Kanan, 2014; Pissuwan, Gazzana, Mongkolsuk, & Cortie, 2020). These gold nanomaterials have localized surface plasmon resonance properties. This effect occurs when free electrons on the surface of gold nanomaterials are excited by incident light and form a characteristic resonance peak in the UV–visible region. The position and shape of this absorption peak will change with factors such as particle spacing, size, and surrounding environment. Therefore, they can be used as an excellent signal transduction unit, and a wide variety of fluorescent colorimetric sensors can be prepared by modifying gold nanomaterials and endowing them with functions (Rakshit, Moulik, & Bhattacharya, 2017; Upadhyayula, 2012). This assay is low cost, simple and fast. Given the high-quality properties of gold nanomaterials, numerous researchers have based gold nanomaterials on detecting pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices.

In recent years, fluorescent probes have attracted much attention in the bio-sensing and chemical analysis fields (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2017). Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) are one of the excellent candidates as fluorescent probes because of their high stability, bright fluorescence and good biocompatibility (Luo, et al., 2017). In view of the above, Yan et al. developed a fluorescent probe for the detection of E. coli using bovine serum albumin (BSA)-modified AuNCs (Fig. 2 ). Because Cu2+ can burst the fluorescence of AuNCs, in the absence of E. coli, Cu2+ synergizes with BSA, resulting in the fluorescence burst of AuNCs; when E. coli is added, because E. coli can reduce Cu2+ to Cu+, it cannot synergize with BSA and AuNCs fluorescence is restored. The method has a LOD of 89.0 CFU mL−1 for E. coli within 30.0 min (Yan, et al., 2018). The above examples use AuNCs as markers for detecting pathogenic bacteria, but it still shows a high LOD and is unable to detect some pathogens with a low infection load (e.g. E. coli O157:H7, sometimes as low as 10 cells). To solve this problem, Cheng et al. prepared nanocapsules with gold nanoclusters encapsulated with chitosan and combined fluorescent nanocapsules labeling with immunomagnetic separation to specifically identify E. coli O157:H7 cells through antibody-antigen interactions. Antibodies that can capture E. coli O157:H7 are first modified on the surface of the nanocapsules, while antibodies that capture the target strain are modified on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles. Magnetic separation was performed when both nanoparticles captured and recognized the target strain from the sample. The fluorescence intensity of the gold nanocluster-embedded chitosan (AuNCs@CS) nanocapsules attached to the bacterial cells was measured, resulting in quantitative measurements of the target strains. Because more fluorescent AuNCs can be attached to each bacterial cell, the fluorescence signal can be significantly amplified, and E. coli O157:H7 can be detected down to 1.0 CFU mL−1. The method has been successfully used for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 in drinking water and milk samples (Cheng, et al., 2018).

In addition to the above strategies, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is also an important method in fluorescence detection and often involves the conversion of electronic interactions among energy donor-acceptor pairs (Cui, Perepichka, MacLeod, & Rosei, 2020). Because of their unique properties, gold nanoparticles are often used as energy FRE receptors in FRET. As a typical example, Ghayyem et al. used azithromycin (Azm) functionalized CeO2 nanoparticles (Azm-CeO2NPs) and aptamer- AuNPs for T detection of E. coli. Azithromycin binds to the 50S ribosomal subunit of bacteria when the target strain is present, Azm and the specific aptamer bind to the surface of E. coli, leading to the FRET phenomenon, the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased with increasing E. coli concentration. The method can detect E. coli in 30 min with a detection range of 10.0 to 1.5 × 105 CFU mL−1 and a LOD of 1.04 CFU mL−1 (Ghayyem & Faridbod, 2022). Another strategy is that fluorescent nanogold clusters can be synthesized in situ on the surface of the target bacteria after capture, thus enabling the quantification of pathogenic bacteria. For example, Hossein et al. synthesized fluorescent AuNCs on the surface of the bacteria after capturing the target bacteria to detect Listeria monocytogenes. They immobilized leucocin A on the glass surface to capture the target strains, then synthesized fluorescent AuNCs, labeled the target strains and imaged the slides under a fluorescent imager to enable detection. The method is portable, rapid and can detect L. monocytogenes in samples within 45–50 min with the LOD of 2.0 × 105 CFU mL−1, which can be increased by changing the ligands used for stabilizing the AuNCs (Hossein-Nejad-Ariani, et al., 2018).

From the discussed examples, it can be concluded that because of the excellent biocompatibility, ultra-small size and high quantum yield of gold nanomaterials, AuNCs-based fluorescence analysis techniques of have been successfully applied for the detection of pathogenic bacteria in different samples. However, the preparation of polymorphic gold nanomaterials is always controllable under harsh conditions and the nanoparticles tend to agglomerate, which is one of the reasons why gold nanomaterials require more efforts in practical applications (Gorbunova, Apyari, Dmitrienko, & Zolotov, 2020; Wu, Zhu, Weng, Li, & Zhao, 2022). Therefore, fine design and characterization of gold nanomaterials are needed for applications in order to achieve optimization of properties while avoiding particle agglomeration and other adverse effects, and to perform rigorous purification and modification of the particles.

Along with gold nanomaterials, as one of the important and precious new fluorescent metallic nanomaterials, silver nanomaterials, characterized by tunable emission wavelengths, good biocompatibility, ultra-small size, high quantum yield, high luminescence intensity and low toxicity, have been widely studied and applied in many fields, such as catalysis, optics, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, bio- and chemosensing, cytotoxicity, antimicrobial detection, electronics, and dark-field imaging, etc. (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2017). In particular, silver nanoparticles have a high specific surface area due to their size effect, which increases the probability of contact between silver nanoparticles and microbial surfaces, and thus silver nanoparticles have a great potential for development in pathogenic bacteria detection and antimicrobials (Kim, Park, Jeon, Kim, & Seo, 2017). To serve as a model example, Zhang et al. designed a fluorescent assays based on silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) to detect S. typhimurium (Fig. 3 A). They made the first creative use of a triple trigger sequence-regenerative chain displacement amplification and self-protective hairpin templates to generate scaffolded AgNCs, enhanced fluorescence signal through cycling. The detection range of this method is 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 and a LOD is 50.0 CFU mL−1. Notably, this was the first report on the use of synthesized metal nanoclusters for the detection of live bacteria, which can be achieved by changing the corresponding aptamer as a recognition probe for the target to detect different pathogenic bacteria and small molecules (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2017).

Among the many silver nanomaterials, DNA-stabilized silver nanoclusters (DNA - AgNCs) are the most representative Ag-based emitters, due to its unique fluorescent properties such as excellent photophysical properties, low toxicity, stability, good biocompatibility, and strong fluorescence emission, it has been used by many researchers for the detection of pathogenic bacteria (Lyu, Li, Wang, Guo, & Wang, 2019). For example, Zheng et al. used fluorescent silver nanoclusters with DNA as a template to detect E. coli (Fig. 3B). They first prepared a composite system containing magnetic nanoparticles-DNAzyme-acetylcholinesterase (MNP-DNAzyme-AChE), where DNAzyme is the bacterial-specific recognition unit, and when E. coli lysate is present, the DNAzyme substrate is cleaved and AChE is released into the solution. After magnetic separation, the addition of DNA-AgNCs and Acetylthiocholine (ATCh), which was catalyzed by AChE to hydrolyze to thiocholine (TCh), significantly enhanced the fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs. The LOD of the method is 60.0 CFU mL−1, and the detection range is 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1. The method has been successful in detecting E. coli in tap water and milk (Zheng, et al., 2018). Nonetheless, such versatile bioanalytical methods for biomolecules still require complex recognition elements, implying increased complexity in preparation and manipulation. The design and manufacture of these components requires sophisticated techniques and equipment and is often time and resource intensive. To this end, Fu et al. established a fluorescence burst system including magnetic beads, nucleic acid aptamers and AuNPs to detect S. typhimurium using the fluorescence intensity change caused by FRET between DNA-AgNCs and AuNPs (Fig. 3C), thus avoiding the need for complex recognition elements and simplifies the handling and implementation of bioanalytical methods. The method has successfully detected S. typhimurium in artificially contaminated milk in the range of 7.6 × 103 to 7.6 × 106 CFU mL−1 and LOD of 3.4 × 102 CFU mL−1 (Fu, Yang, et al., 2022). On the other hand, Yang et al. constructed a DNA-stabilized silver nanocluster fluorescent platform based on exonuclease III amplification-assisted detection of S. typhimurium (Fig. 3D). Notably, Exo III-based DNA cyclase digestion does not require specific target DNA sequences, which offers a promising option for simplified and rapid implementation of bioanalytical methods. The detection of S. typhimurium is achieved based on the fluorescence changes of DNA-AgNCs. Under optimal conditions, the detection range of the method is 4.6 × 102 to 4.6 × 107 CFU mL−1, and a LOD is 82.0 CFU mL−1 (Yang, et al., 2021).

All of the above examples use DNA-AgNCs as fluorescent nanomaterials, and despite their many excellent properties, the LOD were too high to detect bacteria with small residuals. In order to solve this problem, Zhang et al. established a rapid detection method for E. coli O157:H7 based on immunomagnetic nanoparticles and silver nanocluster probes. An antibody that specifically recognizes E. coli O157:H7 is first combined with magnetic nanoparticles to form an immunomagnetic nanoprobe, followed by the addition of aptamer-modified silver nanoclusters to form a fluorescent silver nanoprobe. The former captures E. coli O157:H7 and then explicitly binds to a silver nanocluster fluorescent probe. The fluorescent silver nanoprobes that do not bind to bacteria are present in the supernatant, and the fluorescence signal in the supernatant is measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, which indirectly determines the quantitative detection of the bacterial population. The detection range of the method is 10.0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1, and a LOD is 6.031 × 10−3 CFU mL−1 in the milk mock sample (Zhang, Liu, et al., 2021).

It is not difficult to see that silver nanomaterials are indeed widely used in pathogenic bacteria detection due to their good biocompatibility, ultra-small size, strong luminescence, low toxicity, etc., and they are cheaper than gold nanomaterials, with obvious cost advantages (Sharma, et al., 2021). However, at the same time, silver nanomaterials also have some limitations and defects. First, silver nanomaterials are susceptible to oxidation and tend to lose their properties. Second, silver nanomaterials are not easy to use in a solvent environment and need to be converted into water-soluble nanomaterials, which increases the complexity of the preparation process. Thirdly, the dispersion and control of silver nanomaterials are difficult issues, and they are prone to form larger particles, which hinders their effectiveness in practical applications. Finally, excessive use of silver nanomaterials is cytotoxic and may lead to biological damage, while their unregulated release in the environment may cause some effects on both aquatic and terrestrial organisms (Hossain, Islam, & Chowdhury, 2022). Therefore, when applying silver nanomaterials, it is necessary to strictly control the amount of use and emissions, while paying attention to their reasonable modification and purification to ensure that the optimization of performance is achieved while avoiding negative effects.

As can be seen from the previous section, gold and silver nanomaterials are used for fluorescence detection of pathogenic bacteria due to their many excellent properties, but gold and silver nanomaterials are expensive as precious metals. Compared to gold and silver nanomaterials, quantum dots are relatively inexpensive and more readily available, and show great potential for fluorescence detection of pathogenic bacteria (Ye, Zheng, et al., 2020). Quantum dots are nanocrystals of semiconductor materials, which are fluorescent spherical nanoparticles with quantum mechanical properties, usually composed of elements of groups II-VI or III-V of the periodic table, ranging in size from 2 to 10 nm, they can radiate different colors depending on their size (Klostranec & Chan, 2006). Compared with traditional organic fluorescent materials, quantum dots have unique properties such as narrow emission bands, brighter fluorescence, resistance to photobleaching, and excellent optical properties, which facilitate the simultaneous imaging of multiple fluorophores (Resch-Genger, Grabolle, Cavaliere-Jaricot, Nitschke, & Nann, 2008). Due to the quantum confinement effect, the band gap can be tuned by controlling the size and material composition of the quantum dots. Therefore, it has good fluorescence luminescence properties and has been used as a fluorescent probe and fluorescent donor in fluorescent assays (Sutarlie, Ow, & Su, 2017). In recent years, some novel fluorescent assays based on QDs have been reported for the detection of pathogenic bacteria.

Recently, cellulose-based materials have been widely used in analytical chemistry as a platform for immunodiagnostic and chromatographic techniques (Bodelon, et al., 2014). Based on this, Tang et al. used QDs as a fluorescent nanobioprobe on a specially designed cellulose-based swab (solid-phase enrichment system) to establish a specific, efficient, and ultrasensitive assays for Salmonella. The method can detect Salmonella within 2 days (Tang, et al., 2016). But the method takes too long to detect. To reduce detection time and increase detection sensitivity, many researchers use immunomagnetic separation (IMS) to enrich the analyte concentration. Dogan et al. used chitosan-modified CdTe quantum dots to construct fluorescent markers that were combined with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles for the detection of E. coli. Antibodies specific for E. coli are first combined with two nanoparticles and incubated. The magnetic nanoparticles were used as E. coli capture probes, and once the E. coli was captured, secondary antibody-bound quantum dots were attached to the bacterial surface and then washed for fluorometric measurements. The detection time of the method for E. coli is within 120.0 min, and the detection range is 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 108 CFU mL−1, and a LOD is 30.0 CFU mL−1 (Dogan, et al., 2016). Unfortunately, as in the above examples, most QDs were synthesized under harsh conditions, such as the use of toxic reagents, heavy metals, and complex procedures. In contrast, DNA-QDs only through the interaction of DNA bases can be synthesized under green, simple hydrothermal conditions (Guo, et al., 2013). Inspired by the above, Wang et al. combined gold nanoparticles with DNA quantum dots to construct a novel self-assembled core-shell structured fluorescent probe (DNA-QDs/AuNPs, DQA) for the first time, which was combined with IMS to detect S. typhimurium. Using a limited number of sites on the bacteria to specifically bind a large number of DNA-QDs, the fluorescence intensity of individual bacteria is enhanced without the need for cumbersome aptamer modifications. The detection range of the method is 10.0 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1, and a LOD for S. typhimurium is 13.6 CFU mL−1 (Wang, Li, et al., 2020). However, IMS usually requires manual manipulation, making the experimental process cumbersome and unsuitable for improving the detection sensitivity of large quantities (10 mL or more) of target bacteria. To overcome this problem, Xue et al. established the fluorescent assays using high-gradient magnetic separation and QDs for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 (Fig. 4 ), which can be used to detect large quantities of bacteria in quantities larger than 10 mL. First, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with E. coli O157:H7 monoclonal antibodies modified on the surface in the presence of high-gradient magnetic fields (HGMFs) captured the target strain to form magnetic bacteria. Then immune QDs were used to react with them to form complexes. Finally, the ligands were fluorescently detected with a fluorescence detection device, and the target bacteria were quantified in this way. The method can detect E. coli O157:H7 within 120.0 min with a LOD as low as 14.0 CFU mL−1 (Xue, et al., 2018).

At the same time, some researchers had noted individual QDs have low fluorescence quantum yields due to their susceptibility to external impurities and lattice defects. To overcome such drawbacks, Hu et al. used polymeric nanoparticles as carriers based on QDs and assembled fluorescent nanoparticles (QDNS) layer by layer to detect S. typhimurium. QDNS not only inherits the easy handling capability and colloidal stability of polymer nanoparticles, but also exhibits excellent fluorescence properties. When the target strain was present in the sample, the immuno-QDNS was used to capture the target strain and the “T” and “C” fluorescence lines could be seen simultaneously under the UV lamp (C is the control line; T is the test line). When the target strain was not present in the sample, only the “C” line was visible under the UV lamp. The method showed 100% accuracy in 50 tests and could detect S. typhimurium within 10.0 min. The range of detection in broth is 5.0 × 104 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 and a LOD is 5.0 × 104 CFU mL−1 (Hu, et al., 2020). Compared with individual QDs, the fluorescence quantum yield of QDNS is significantly improved, which is expected to be widely used in the future.

QDs have the advantages of narrow emission bands and brighter fluorescence, because of the high fluorescence emission efficiency have been more used in small molecule detection. The core-shell structure of QDs can be easily modified by antibodies, aptamers and other biomolecules, thus improving the accuracy of their detection, but some QDs such as CdSe, CdTe, PbS are prone to secondary pollution to the environment because of the heavy metal ions contained in them, and the high synthesis cost also limits their application (Manshian, et al., 2017; Tsoi, Dai, Alman, & Chan, 2013; Wang, Xie, Zhao, Wang, & Wang, 2009). Therefore, when using QDs, we need to pay attention to the reasonable modification and purification of QDs, and at the same time, we need to pay attention to reducing the negative impact on the environment and strictly control the amount of use. At the same time, it is also necessary to actively explore more environmentally friendly, economical and practical alternatives to promote the continuous development and improvement of QDs technology.

Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are hexagonal NaYF4 nanocrystals doped by rare earths, and their luminescent color can be changed by changing the type of rare earth doping (Jia, Cong, Shen, & Yu, 2019). Unlike conventional fluorescent materials and quantum dots, UCNPs can convert lower energy NIR excitation light to higher energy visible or ultraviolet light, resulting in a large anti-Stokes shift (Feng, et al., 2015; Zhang, Liu, et al., 2021). In the past few years, it has become a popular research topic in the field of pathogenic bacteria detection due to its low toxicity, low fluorescence background, narrow emission wavelength, tiny optical drift, and easy functionalization modification and long luminescence lifetime (DaCosta, Doughan, Han, & Krull, 2014; Fan, Liu, & Zhang, 2019; Liang et al., 2020; Zhu, Zhang, Liu, & Zhang, 2019).

As an example, Liu et al. designed an aptamer-based sensor using aptamer-conjugated magnetic nanoparticles for recognition and concentration, as well as aptamer-conjugated UCNPs for fluorescence assays, which successfully detected L. monocytogene (Liu, et al., 2021). They also established a FRET fluorescence sensing platform to detect S. aureus using UCNPS-aptamer as donor and black hole burst 1 (BHQ-1) with cDNA attached as acceptor (Fig. 5 ). When there is no target strain in the system, the cDNA nucleotide chain complementarily paired with the S. aureus aptamer, bringing the UCNPs closer to BHQ-1, which bursts the fluorescence emitted by the UCNPS. When a target strain is present in the system, the aptamer dissociated from cDNA-BHQ-1 and bound to S. aureus, leading to the recovery of the burst fluorescence. The detection range of the method is 3.6 × 101 to 3.6 × 107 CFU mL−1 with a LOD of 6.0 CFU mL−1. The method has been successful in detecting S. aureus in spiked bottled water, milk, tea power and fish (Liu, Yu, et al., 2022). The above example was a detection of a particular pathogenic bacteria, which is not characterized by diversity and a wide range of detection, in order to solve this problem. Wu et al. doped different rare earth luminescent ions into the nanoparticles, and the different rare earth ion-doped UCNPs have independent emission peaks that can be used to label the corresponding target bacteria. Three aptamers that specifically recognize pathogenic bacteria were then attached to these multicolor UCNPs, and magnetic nanoparticles were attached to one end of the RNA sequence complementary to the aptamer to prepare detection probes. Once the nanomaterials encountered the target strain, the RNA sequences with magnetic nanoparticles dissociated from the upconverted nanomaterials and were magnetically separated for fluorescence intensity detection. The simultaneous detection of S. aureus, Vibrio parahaemolyticus and S. typhimurium was achieved with a LOD of 25.0 CFU mL−1, 10.0 CFU mL−1 and 15.0 CFU mL−1, respectively (Wu, et al., 2014).

All of the above are analytical methods based on the integration of UCNP with aptamers. However, these methods are still very challenging, especially in detecting multiple bacteria simultaneously. At the same time, most aptamer molecules still suffer from high manufacturing cost, poor stability, stringent storage conditions, and short shelf life (Magennis, et al., 2016). It is worth noting that the prevalent focusing effects and electrostatic interactions of microorganisms (Tokonami & Iida, 2017). Bacteria as a widespread natural material, usually carry a net negative surface charge, and there are multivalent interactions between UCNPs materials and bacteria. Based on this property, Yin et al. designed a fluorescent sensor consisting of guanidinium-based functionalized upconversion fluorescent nanoparticles (UCNPs@GDN), tannic acid and hydrogen peroxide (HP) to achieve the detection of seven common food-borne bacteria through electrostatic interactions. The detection range of the method is 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 108 CFU mL−1, and the LOD is 1.3 × 102 CFU mL−1 (Yin, et al., 2019). Moreover, also based on this strategy, they designed and synthesized three surfaces chemically modified UCNPs probes composed of boric acid, quaternary ammonium salt or phosphate groups to construct a novel upconversion fluorescent sensor array for quantitative analysis of seven common foodborne pathogenic bacteria, which can be well identified and classified according to their Gram status (Yin, Jing, Li, Deng, & Wang, 2020).

With the advantages of low toxicity and unique luminescence properties, UCNPs have dominated the detection of a wide range of bacteria. However, the fabrication of multifunctional identification platforms based on UCNPs is relatively complicated with long preparation time, while the quantum yield of hydrophilic upconversion nanomaterials is low. This problem needs to be urgently improved in order to enhance their application in practical assays (Shen, et al., 2021). Currently, for these problems, some researchers have proposed strategies such as adjusting the size and surface potential of the particles and changing the structure and morphology of the materials to increase their hydrophilicity and quantum efficiency. In addition, there are researchers introducing new nanomaterials and modifying them to optimize their properties and applications. It is believed that in the near future, through the efforts of these scientists, the UCNPs technology will be further developed and improved to provide a more accurate and effective tool for the detection of pathogenic bacteria (Chen & Wang, 2020; Wilhelm, 2017).

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have been widely studied in the field of biochemical analysis in recent years because of their superparamagnetic properties, excellent monodispersity, and fast separation speed (Cardoso, et al., 2018; Di et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2020). The surface of MNPs can be easily modified by multiple receptors and the MNPs can act as ferromagnetic markers, which can be easily separated from the components of the mixture with the help of a magnet and can be precisely aggregated at the desired location by an applied magnetic field (Megens & Prins, 2005). Based on such properties, MNPs can be used as an excellent carrier for magnetic immunoseparation and concentration of pathogens in the field of microbiological analysis, being used in fluorescent assays (Lim, et al., 2016; Olsvik et al., 1994; Qiu, Zhou, Chen, & Lin, 2009; Schaumburg, Carrell, & Henry, 2019).

For example, Zong et al. reported a multichannel magnetic fluorescent nanosensor (dMFN) for bacterial detection. The system consists of quaternary magnetic nanoparticles combined with three different fluorescent proteins (FPs). When bacteria are added to the mixture to interact with it, characteristic levels of the three FPs are replaced by the outer membrane component of the pathogen, producing a fluorescent response. After immunomagnetic separation, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant is measured to indirectly quantify the bacterial concentration. The method resulted in an identification rate of 89.7% within 30 min for bacterial identification (Zong, et al., 2019). However, most organic fluorophores tend to be unstable and have low fluorescence quantum yields. To overcome this drawback, immunomagnetic beads (IMBs) are always combined with luminescent nanomaterials, especially quantum dots of luminescent nanomaterials, to form composite structures that detect the level of bacteria by measuring the change of their fluorescence emission intensity (Cheng, et al., 2016; Wang, Li, Wang, & Slavik, 2011). As a typical example, Hao et al. designed a fluorescent sensor based on magnetic nanoparticles, quantum dot labeling, and MnO2 nanosignal amplification for fast and sensitive detection of S. typhimurium. The detection range of the method is 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 and a LOD is 43.0 CFU mL−1 (Hao, et al., 2020). But the size of IMBs is usually much larger than that of quantum dots, hindering the effective luminescence space of quantum dots in the formed complexes and thus affecting the effective output of the luminescent quantum dot fluorescence signal, thus reducing the detection sensitivity, which needs to be improved by people's efforts.

In general, MNPs can be classified as pure metals, metal oxides and magnetic nanocomposites. The most commonly used nanoparticles for biosensing are Co, Fe, Ti, Ni metal alloys and iron oxide. Among them, iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O 4 ) have been widely studied for their sufficiently high magnetic moment, chemical stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Karimi, et al., 2014; Karimi, Karimi, & Shokrollahi, 2013; Kaur, Singh, & Randhawa, 2014; Maleki, Simchi, Imani, & Costa, 2012; Pulisova, Kovac, Voigt, & Raschman, 2013). For example, Yu et al. developed a fluorescent assays based on magnetic nano-Fe3O4 and hybridized chain reaction (HCR) (Fig. 6 A). Designed primers specific for Salmonella produced ssDNA by aPCR and were captured by biotin probe-labeled magnetic beads (MBs), two hairpin probes of ssDNA triggered the HCR amplification, and Fe3O4 separated the ssDNA and thus measured the fluorescence intensity. The detection limit of this method for Salmonella in buffer solution was 74.0 CFU mL−1 (Yu, et al., 2019). However, iron oxide nanoparticles are prone to oxidation and corrosion, which affects the accuracy of the assay. To solve the aforementioned problem, some research groups have used carbon dots to wrap iron oxide nanoparticles, which can prevent oxidation and corrosion of iron oxide nanoparticles due to the better dispersion and stability of carbon dots to them (Pal, Pal, Jimenez, & Perez-Rodriguez, 2012). As a typical example, Mukesh et al. wrapped magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles with carbon dots to form Mag-CDs, which were further functionalized with amino groups (chitosan) (Fig. 6B). When the target strain present, the target strain was strongly immobilized on the mixed material and separated from the sample with an external magnet for fluorescence detection. The method successfully detected E. coli and S. aureus in the samples with a LOD of 3.5 × 102 CFU mL−1 and 3.0 × 102 CFU mL−1 for them, respectively (Bhaisare, et al., 2016). The fluorescent materials and magnetic nanospheres in the above examples can only provide one or two signals, which cannot simultaneously meet the requirements of different medical conditions. To this end, Hu et al. prepared colorimetric-fluorescent magnetic nanospheres (CFMNs) as labels for lateral flow immunoassay (LFIA) by assembling Fe3O4 and QDs on the surface of polymeric nanospheres for the detection of S. typhimurium. The detection range of the method for S. typhimurium is 1.88 × 104 to 1.88 × 107 CFU mL−1, and a LOD is 3.75 × 103 CFU mL−1. The method can provide colorimetric and magnetic signals for both visual readout and quantitative detection of S. typhimurium. Moreover, the multi-signal readout allows the detection method to be flexibly applied to areas with different conditions (Hu, et al., 2019).

It is worth noting that most of the fluorescent probes used in the above methods are subject to interference from ground signals and light in the analysis of biological samples, which is not conducive to achieving high sensitivity in bioassays. To this end, Zhu et al. established a fluorescent alkaline earth sulfide nanoprobe (AES nanoprobe) based on the functionalization of two-color sulfide fluorescent nanoparticles with target bacteria. Suspensions of S. aureus and E. coli were incubated with AES probes and magnetic ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles (ZFO NPs), respectively, then separated and washed with a magnetic field, and bacterial aggregates at the tube walls fluoresced bright red and blue, respectively. The detection range of the method is 25.0 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1, and a LOD for S. aureus and E. coli are 21.0 CFU mL−1 and 28.0 CFU mL−1, respectively. The AES nanoprobe emits stronger and more competitive fluorescence than normal probes, improving the sensitivity of bacterial fluorescence detection. Notably, this method makes it possible to determine both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains simultaneously. (Zhu, et al., 2020). In addition this, because the three-dimensional distribution of bacteria in aqueous solution may overlap or block the fluorescence signal, resulting in a reduction in the number of bacteria detected, and converting the distribution of fluorescent bacteria from three-dimensional (spatial) to two-dimensional (planar) can improve the accuracy. To address this problem, Hu et al. combined a low-gradient magnetic field with a fast regional convolutional neural network (R–CNN) deep learning algorithm to build a fluorescent assays for the detection of S. typhimurium. The method is able to detect S. typhimurium within 2.5 h. The detection range is 6.9 to 1.1 × 103 CFU mL−1 and a LOD is 55.0 CFU mL−1 (Hu, et al., 2021).

Although MNPs have done a lot of research and achieved a lot of results in the fluorescence detection of bacteria, there is still a lot of work to be done. First, new synthetic techniques and methods need to be developed to prepare novel MNPs with good biocompatibility. Second, many MNPs are toxic, so it is also important to find new ways to reduce the cytotoxicity and improve the biocompatibility of currently available MNPs. Finally, MNPs are not self-fluorescent and must be labeled with fluorescent dyes or quantum dots for biosensing applications, and the recoverability of MNPs is not always guaranteed (Thabet, Abdel-Moamen, & Abdelhady, 2016; Yilmaz, Sarp, Uzcan, Ozalp, & Soylak, 2021).

Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials include carbon dots (CDs), polydopamine (PDA), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene oxide (GO), and nanographite (NG), etc. Owing to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility, carbon nanomaterials have attracted great attention in constructing efficient biocompatibility analysis of various chemical or biological compounds (Cui, Ye, Ping, & Sun, 2020; Maiti, Tong, Mou, & Yang, 2019; Patel, Singh, & Kim, 2019). Compared to conventional quantum dots and organic fluorescent materials, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials have many other advantages, such as easy functionalization, robust chemical inertness and high resistance to photobleaching (Cha, Shin, Annabi, Dokmeci, & Khademhosseini, 2013; Li, Wang, Li, Feng, & Feng, 2019; Shen et al., 2023). Carbon based nanomaterials are usually biodegradable and therefore more environmentally friendly. Thus, fluorescent carbon nanomaterials can be used as fluorescent probes or fluorescent donors, providing a new means of detecting pathogenic bacteria (Shi, et al., 2019).

CDs are amorphous or crystalline quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm. In recent years, it has received increasing attention in the development of fluorescent assays due to its easy surface modification, good biocompatibility, simple preparation, and good solubility (Du, Xu, Fan, Sun, & Peng, 2019; C. L. Shen, Lou, Liu, Dong, & Shan, 2020; Wu, Liu, & Yuan, 2017; Zhu et al., 2015). For example, Yang et al. used organosilicon shells wrapped around carbon dots to form nanocapsules CDs@BONs, on the surface of which antibodies against S. aureus were bound for specific capture of the target strains. After magnetic separation and NaBH4 reduction, CDs were released from the BONs and then detected by fluorescence. This method can detect S. aureus in the range of 1.0–2.0 × 102 CFU mL−1 with a LOD of 1.0 CFU mL−1. The method has been applied to the detection of S. aureus in milk and orange juice (Yang, Liu, et al., 2018). However, the CDs surface was difficult to modify, which affected its further application. In order to avoid the complicated modification work on the surface of carbon dots, the synthesis of carbon dots can be carried out for the specific properties of the target bacteria surface. As a typical example, Zheng et al. used receptor-functionalized CDs as signal readers to build fluorescent sensors to detect a variety of bacteria. The CDs were first modified with three types of vancomycin, boric acid and polymyxin that recognize different bacterial surfaces, allowing the CDs to capture a variety of pathogens with different affinities. They can then bind to bacterial surfaces to form bacterial-CDs couples, which cause a burst of fluorescence from the CDs. Because different bacteria have different affinities with this sensor, resulting in different levels of fluorescence burst, strains can be classified and discriminated. Using this method, six strains of E. coli, Vibrio desulfuricans, Staphylococcus squirrelis, Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa could be identified with an accuracy of 91.6%, which is promising for multipathogenic bacteria analysis (Zheng, et al., 2019).

As mentioned earlier, antibodies based on immune response have high specificity but are costly and not reproducible, in contrast to aptamers, which are less expensive. However, aptamers are prone to degradation and assays using a single aptamer as a recognition element are less specific (Yu, et al., 2017). On the other hand, most fluorescent assays are single-emission, and single-emission fluorescent assays are more susceptible to interference from a variety of factors unrelated to the analyte (Huang, et al., 2018). To address these issues, several researchers have worked on combining aptamers and antibiotics as dual recognition elements with proportional fluorescence assays. As a typical example, Shen et al. developed the broad-spectrum glycopeptide antibiotic vancomycin (Van) and aptamer-based dual-recognition CDs nanoprobe with controlled G-quadruplex release for ratiometric fluorescence assays of pathogenic bacteria in a rapid and precise manner (Shen, Wu, et al., 2022). In addition this, they also combined recognition aptamers and the Van with quantum dots to form fluorescent Apt-Van-QDs complexes that act as energy acceptors and bind to blue CDs that act as energy donors. Ratiometric fluorescence detection of S. aureus was achieved by terminating the FRET phenomenon (Fig. 7 A). The detection time of the method is 30.0 min with a LOD of 1.0 CFU mL−1 (Shen, Wu, et al., 2020). The application of ratiometric fluorescence provides built-in self-calibration, thereby reducing interference from a variety of non-target related factors. However, the QDs that act as energy receptors in this strategy were somewhat toxic, and some researchers considered whether this could be changed by replacing the receptor material. To this end, Fu et al. encapsulated blue carbon dots (BCDs) in a silica core as the reference signal readout and AuNCs covalently attached to the silica surface as the response signal readout, and used nanocomposites (BCD@SiO2@AuNC) as a proportional fluorescent sensor to achieve selective detection of Gram-negative bacteria (Fig. 7B). The method has a detection range of 1.0 × 103 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 for E. coli and a LOD of 1.5 × 102 CFU mL−1 (Fu, Yang, et al., 2022). AuNCs have better stability and biocompatibility as receptor-responsive signaling readout elements compared to QDs.

Mesoporous polydopamine (PDA) is a nanomaterial combining mesoporous structure and chemical properties of dopamine, which can be obtained by carbonizing it in an inert atmosphere. As a type of carbon-based materials, they have emerged in recent years as a potential material for fluorescence assays of pathogenic bacteria because of their tunable diameter, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, and broad absorption bands from the ultraviolet (UV) to the near-infrared (NIR) region (Pan, Yin, et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2018). As a typical example, Ye et al. established a stimulus-responsive nanoprobe, PDANSs-FAM-Apt, based on polydopamine nanospheres (PDANS), which successfully detected S. aureus at the single-cell level. Specifically, when the target strain was absent, the occurrence of an ultra-efficient FRET efficiency of the energy donor FAM to the acceptor PDANSs caused a fluorescence burst. When the target strain was present, its specific affinity for the aptamer could trigger a reduction in π-π stacking interactions and release of free FAM-Apt, leading to fluorescence recovery. The method has a LOD of 1.0 CFU mL−1 (Ye, Zheng, et al., 2020). The previous method achieved good results, but the detection time was too long for real samples. To solve this problem, Shen et al. synthesized a novel nanozyme Fe@PDA by doping Fe into polydopamine. And it was modified with aptamer, combined with Van functionalized Fe3O4, and the colorimetric fluorescence dual-mode detection of L. monocytogenes was successfully achieved by using magnetic separation technology and TMB color development technology. The detection time of the method is 20.0 min, and the detection range of the colorimetric method is 6.9 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 with a LOD of 2.3 CFU mL−1. The detection range of the fluorescence method is 3.0 to 1.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 with a LOD of 1.0 CFU mL−1 (Shen, Wu, et al., 2022).

Nanofibers (NFs) are excellent fluorescent nanomaterials that are commonly used in pathogenic bacteria detection due to their unique properties such as flexibility, high surface area to volume ratio, high porosity and very small pore size (Pebdeni, Sadri, & Pebdeni, 2016; Yang, Liu, et al., 2018). Conventional fluorescent NFs are mostly developed with aptamers as specific recognition factor and merged with semiconductor quantum dots, but they contain toxic heavy metals. CQDs were recently appeared as a new type of carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm, due to their high specific surface area, quantum effect and low toxicity, the above problems were solved. As a typical example, Pebdeni et al. surface modified NFs with CQDs to establish an amplified fluorescent biosensing platform for the detection of S. aureus. The method has a detection range of 10.0 to 1.0 × 108 CFU mL−1 for S. aureus and a LOD of 10.0 CFU mL−1 (Pebdeni, et al., 2022). Compared to conventional methods, CQDs do not have any cytotoxicity and have good biocompatibility. In recent years, as a type of CQDs, GQDs also have gradually become idealized carbon nanomaterials due to their unique properties such as easy preparation and high quantum yield. The unique optical properties of GQDs make them promising fluorescent probes for the detection of bacteria using fluorescent assays (Sutarlie, et al., 2017). As a typical example, Safardoust-Hojaghan et al. used graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensors for the detection of S. aureus and E. coli. The method is easy to operate and can detect the target strains within 2.0 h. The detection range is from 0.0 to 9.0 × 107 CFU mL−1 (Safardoust-Hojaghan, et al., 2017).

Compared to GQDs, GO is often considered as an important bursting agent for the construction of different fluorescent assays because of the very high fluorescence bursting efficiency of GO, which can interact non-healingly with single-stranded ssDNA probes through hydrophobic and π-π stacking interactions between graphene oxide hexagonal cells and nucleotide bases. Therefore, GO based fluorescent assays has been widely used (Liu, et al., 2018; Sadeghi, Mohsenzadeh, Abnous, Taghdisi, & Ramezani, 2018; Yang, Liu, et al., 2018). As a typical example, Bahari et al. developed a fluorescent aptamer sensor for detection of A. baumannii using o-phenylenediamine carbon dots (o-CD), nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (NCND) as donors and GO as acceptors, based on FRET between them. The detection range of the method is 2.0 × 103 to 4.5 × 107 CFU mL−1 and a LOD is 3.0 × 102 CFU mL−1 (Bahari, et al., 2021). NG is also an outstanding carbon nanomaterial, similar to GO, with an ultra-high specific surface area that can bind a large number of molecules, including nucleic acids, proteins, liposomes, and certain drugs, by physical adsorption or chemical coupling (Duan, Ning, Song, & Deng, 2014; Lu, Chen, Wang, Zheng, & Li, 2015; Perez-Lopez & Merkoci, 2012). In addition, NG is a good nano-bursting agent that is easier and less costly to produce compared to GO. Therefore, NG is promising in fluorescent assays and can be used as a detection platform for bacteria (Wei, Zhang, Wang, & Duan, 2015). As a typical example, He et al. established a fluorescent assays to detect Streptococcus enteritidis using NG as a fluorescent bursting agent and circular signal amplification using deoxyribonuclease I (DNase I). The method has a detection range of 1.0 × 102 to 1.0 × 106 CFU mL−1 for S. enteritidis and LOD of 50.0 CFU mL−1. The method has been successfully applied to the detection of S. enteritidis in milk (Chen, Lin, et al., 2017).

With the above examples, various carbon-based nanomaterials have shown promising applications in the detection of disease-causing bacteria due to their excellent optical properties. However, there are still many challenges for practical applications, and the surface of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials that are not easily modified and the clarification of the luminescence mechanism of carbon-based nanomaterials are among the main challenges currently faced (Abraham & Balachandran, 2022). Apart from that, the recent reports have only focused on the construction of various sensing strategies based on carbon-based nanomaterials (Hu, Wujcik, Kelarakis, Cyriac, & Gong, 2017; Pan, Yin, et al., 2019). More work is needed to move from scientific research to the practical application of environmental monitoring. In the future, the development of carbon nanomaterial sensors with high selectivity, high sensitivity, high reproducibility and high stability should be the primary direction of efforts, which should be integrated into microfluidics and portable sensor chips to give them the ability to detect the point of care, so as to establish an early warning mechanism of pathogenic bacteria pollution, and ultimately protect human health.

Organic Frameworks are one of the most rapidly developing three-dimensional porous materials in the 21st century, of which the well-known metal-organic-frameworks (MOFs) is one of the leaders. In the last decade, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have also begun to emerge (Freund, et al., 2021). As a new class of nanomaterials, MOFs consisting of metal nodes (metal ions or clusters) coordinated with organic linkers have received special attention due to their attractive features such as high porosity, high surface area (Li, Chen, & Pan, 2021), and excellent stability, and have been successfully applied in bacterial detection (Wang, et al., 2017). In addition to this, COFs consisting of covalently linked organic structural units are also excellent materials for pathogenic bacteria detection due to their high crystallinity, tunable pore parameters, excellent chemical and thermal stability (Li, et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2022).

As a typical example, Gupta et al. first reported fluorescence detection of E. coli using water-dispersible terbium MOFs (Tb-MOFs). Tb-BTC was biointeracted with anti-E. coli antibodies and then studied as an E. coli biosensor. The detection range of the method was in the range of 1.3 × 102 to 1.3 × 108 CFU mL−1, with a LOD of 3.0 CFU mL−1 and a detection time of 5.0 min (Gupta, et al., 2020). However, this application was limited by the relatively low water stability of Tb-MOFs in terms of sensor shelf life. Phages are known to be natural enemies of bacteria and represent an important class of biorecognition elements that remain highly stable under a variety of conditions while allowing people to distinguish between live and dead bacteria (Hagens & Loessner, 2007). With regards this, Bhardwaj et al. bound a MOF named NH2-MIL-53(Fe) to phage by using glutaraldehyde as a cross-linking agent, successfully realized high sensitivity detection of S. aureus in synthetic and real samples. The detection range of the method was 40.0 to 4.0 × 108 CFU mL−1 with a LOD of 31.0 CFU mL−1. The method has significant advantages in terms of specificity, reproducibility, extended range of linear detection and stability in long-term storage (Bhardwaj, Bhardwaj, Nayak, et al., 2017). Furthermore, Li et al. developed a novel Cd-MOF-based sensor for the detection of S. albus by fluorescence enhancement. The method is also capable of detecting nitroantibiotics and chlortetracycline simultaneously (Li, et al., 2022). Guo et al. successfully provided a simple, environmentally friendly and low-cost method for the rapid detection of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (PA) in water by detecting 2-aminoacetophenone (2-AA), a metabolite of pathogenic PA, using europium metal-organic skeleton (Eu-MOF) as a luminescent sensing material (Guo, et al., 2021).

From the above introduction, it can be seen that MOFs have many excellent properties. However, the emission intensities of most reported MOFs are usually difficult to meet the detection requirements in real samples compared with other well-designed fluorescent probes. It has been reported that COFs can be modified by biomolecules or a variety of functional chemicals (Ding, et al., 2011). All these factors make COFs suitable as carriers for biomolecules or surface modifications. Inspired by the above, Li et al. designed a broad-spectrum probe (MOF@COF-BA) by generating a boronic acid COF layer on the MOF core. Functionalization of COF not only enhances the fluorescence performance but also provides sufficient boric acid. In this design, aptamer-coated nanoparticles were used to capture multidrug-resistant bacteria (MDRB), and because of the interaction between the boric acid moiety and the glycolipids on the bacterial cells, the fluorescent probe MOF@COF-BA was tightly anchored on the surface of MDRB. The method achieved rapid and specific detection of methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) and Acinetobacter baumannii (AB) in the range of 20.0 to 1.0 × 108 CFU mL−1 with an LOD of 7.0 CFU mL−1 (MRSA) and 5.0 CFU mL−1 (AB), respectively (Li, Xu, et al., 2023). Compared with MOFs/COFs alone, MOF@COF hybrid materials have more promising applications.

Compared with traditional materials, MOFs and COFs have many unique properties, such as excellent fluorescence burst performance and high sensitivity. By utilizing the fluorescence bursting properties of MOFs and COFs, high sensitivity detection of bacteria can be achieved. Therefore, MOFs and COFs have a wide range of applications as a potential luminescent material in bacterial detection. However, as an emerging crystalline material, MOFs and COFs still have some challenges and problems. One of them is their stability problem. Due to the complex structure of MOFs and COFs, their stability tends to be low, and they are prone to problems such as structural disintegration or ligand bond breaking, which affects their application performance. In addition, their preparation and synthesis technologies are facing some challenges, such as how to improve the preparation efficiency and reduce the preparation cost. Therefore, the next hotspot for researchers should be to improve the stability and preparation efficiency of MOFs and COFs, and to reduce the preparation cost.

Contamination of food-related matrices by pathogenic bacteria is a hot issue of global public health concern. Rapid and sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria is essential for environmental safety and human health. The development of a rapid, accurate, environmentally friendly, and low-cost assay to report infection levels of pathogenic bacteria in the environment and food-related matrices is one of the useful methods to establish an effective early warning mechanism to avoid jeopardizing food safety and public health, but it is challenging. As an emerging optical detection technology, the fluorescence assays based on nanomaterials have the advantages of fast detection speed and relatively low detection cost compared with the traditional bacterial detection methods. In order to promote further development in this field, we herein reviewed the progress of research on the identification of nanomaterials with pathogenic bacteria and the detection of pathogenic bacteria by functionalized nanoparticles using fluorescent assays. Although nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays have advantages over traditional detection methods in analysis, its application still requires attention to the following issues: (1) The development of fluorescent assays should pursue low prices and simple structures. Currently, fluorescent assays are still in the laboratory research stage. How to transform it into a portable test like colloidal gold test strips and blood glucose testers has become the focus of researchers. For example, combining it with smart products such as smartphones. (2) The development of fluorescent assays should follow environmental principles and it is necessary to assess their environmental safety and permission for use by regulatory authorities. More attention should be paid to post-use disposal as well as recycling issues. (3) The detection of multiplexed bacteria and the regeneration of the sensors are important directions for subsequent research, and the selection and optimization of suitable biometric elements and nanomaterials combined with longer shelf life and better regeneration conditions, to detect all types of pathogenic contaminants in food and environment. (4) The focus of this research area should also be on developing assays that can provide high specificity under a range of harsh environmental conditions, preventing environmental issues from affecting their fluorescence intensity and fluorescence lifetime. (5) When testing samples, detection is always inaccurate because of the difficulty in predicting the physical and chemical properties of the various constituents in environment at different points in time and under different conditions, and the limitations on the total amount that can be analyzed from the various samples. There is therefore a need to develop an efficient, high performance, multiplexed high throughput device for in situ detection to reduce the chance of false detection whilst ensuring early detection of pathogenic contaminants in a variety of samples.

With the continuous development in the field of nanomaterials and sensing technology, the use of nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays in the detection of pathogenic bacteria has broken through the limitations of traditional methods, resulting in much shorter detection time and much higher detection sensitivity. We believe that this systematic review will help researchers to better grasp the latest advances in nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays for pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices and to further develop novel, sensitive, rapid and cost-effective assays. Enabling understanding of environmental and food safety, facilitating decision-making to minimize human health risks. At the same time, due to the extensive integration of different disciplines and advances in science and technology, it is entirely possible that novel portable nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays devices for onsite detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices will be commercialized in the future. In this context, f nanomaterials-based fluorescent assays will play a key role in the detection of pathogenic bacteria in food-related matrices, thereby maintaining food safety and human health.

Guojian Wu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Huimin Qiu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing-original draft. Xin Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis.
